title: On the Southward Flight of Dragons, and the Affliction Commonly Styled Ashscale
author: Maester Baelor Targaryen
summary: A scholarly examination of the recent, unexplained southward migration of dragons, and the mysterious dulling condition of their hides provisionally named Ashscale.
content:
Among the many certainties that scholars have long held concerning dragons, few appeared more reliable than the belief that these creatures, though capable of traversing immense distances, seldom abandoned territories favorable to their nature without compelling reason. Their tempers, breeding grounds, and preferred climates have varied throughout the centuries, yet the broad patterns of their movements have remained remarkably constant within the limits of recorded history.

It is therefore with considerable hesitation that I commit the following observations to parchment, for they concern a phenomenon that neither I nor any living scholar can adequately explain. For nearly fifteen years, dragons throughout the known world have demonstrated an increasing inclination to depart from their customary ranges and fly southward. The journeys in question extend far beyond the deserts of Dorne, beyond the Stepstones, beyond even the shores of Lys, into waters and coastlines so distant that few Westerosi vessels venture there with any regularity.

Merchant captains, free traders, pearl divers, and sailors arriving from distant ports have independently described enormous winged creatures descending upon isolated coastlines before disappearing once more toward warmer horizons. The descriptions vary in detail, yet their central observation remains remarkably consistent: dragons are flying farther south than they once did.

As reports multiplied over recent years, another pattern slowly emerged. A surprising number of witnesses described dragons whose scales appeared unusually dull. Their hides, ordinarily possessing a sheen comparable to polished stone or tempered steel, instead reflected light unevenly, as though coated in fine ash. Others reported patches where the scales appeared pale, brittle, or roughened. It was for this reason that several of my learned colleagues adopted the provisional designation Ashscale.

Among the explanations presently debated within learned circles, two have endured longer than the others. The first concerns the physiology of the dragon's scales. I have argued, together with several esteemed colleagues, that dragons produce beneath their scales a natural oily secretion. Ashscale may interfere with that process. Under this hypothesis, dragons seek out exceptional warmth and mineral composition in southern seas to temporarily soothe the irritation caused by the affliction.

The second explanation proceeds from an altogether different foundation. Several respected natural philosophers have proposed that Ashscale is not principally a disorder of the dragon itself, but the consequence of some minute parasite. The exceedingly warm and saline waters lying far beyond the familiar shipping routes of the Narrow Sea may prove hostile to these unseen organisms, weakening whatever burden the dragon carries.

Accounts gathered from dragonkeepers, shepherds, and seasoned mariners consistently describe afflicted dragons as unusually restless. What distinguishes the affliction is not its lethality, but the profound physiological burden it appears to impose upon otherwise formidable creatures.

Among the common folk, the prevailing belief was that the dragons' departure could not be natural. Many declared it a punishment sent by the gods. There were also those of greater learning whose conclusions differed little in substance, arguing that the long Valyrian practice of binding dragons to the service of mankind represented a profound violation of the natural order. According to this view, the southward flight of the dragons was neither illness nor instinct, but a long-delayed correction—the beasts freeing themselves from centuries of unnatural dependence.
